title: Find the Newest Build Logs First
hook: The failing file is usually one of the newest artifacts.
problem: A CI job produced several logs and reports, and you need to quickly locate the most recent build output before reading everything.
command: find artifacts logs -type f \( -name '*.log' -o -name '*.txt' \) -printf '%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM %p\n' | sort -r | head
starting_state: A project directory contains CI logs under artifacts and logs.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The shell prints the newest matching log files first.
when_to_use: Use when a build leaves many logs and you need the most likely failure source.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof of failure cause; open the relevant log after locating it.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A date-sorted list of recent .log and .txt files.

commands: cd /lab/ci-artifacts && find artifacts logs -type f | sort
cd /lab/ci-artifacts && find artifacts logs -type f \( -name '*.log' -o -name '*.txt' \) -printf '%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM %p\n' | sort -r | head

shorts.title: Start with the newest CI log.
shorts.voiceover: When CI drops a pile of artifacts, sort logs by time first. It narrows the search before you start reading.
shorts.caption: Newest logs first.

linkedin.question: When CI fails, do you inspect logs by timestamp or by guesswork?

youtube.description: Use find and sort to locate the newest CI logs before debugging a failed build.

experiments.variant_a: The failing file is usually one of the newest artifacts.
experiments.variant_b: Find the newest CI logs first.

title: Scan Every CI Log for Error Lines
hook: One grep pass can turn a log pile into a failure list.
problem: Multiple CI logs may contain errors, warnings, stack traces, and test failures, but opening each file manually is slow.
starting_state: A CI artifact directory contains text logs.
what_changed: Nothing changes. grep prints matching file names, line numbers, and excerpts.
when_to_use: Use for the first pass over CI logs when the failing stage is unclear.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat every warning-like match as fatal; confirm with the surrounding log context.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Matching error-like lines with file paths and line numbers.

commands: cd /lab/ci-artifacts && grep -RInE 'error|failed|failure|exception|traceback' artifacts logs | cut -d: -f1 | sort -u

shorts.title: Search all CI logs at once.
shorts.voiceover: Do not open ten logs by hand. Search every artifact for error, failed, exception, and traceback first.
shorts.caption: One grep pass, many logs.

linkedin.question: What words do you search for first in a failed CI artifact bundle?

youtube.description: Search CI logs recursively for error and failure indicators with grep.

experiments.variant_a: One grep pass can turn a log pile into a failure list.
experiments.variant_b: Search every CI artifact for failure words.

title: Show Context Around the First Error
hook: The line before the error often explains the error.
problem: A log contains a clear error line, but the useful cause may be several lines above or below it.
starting_state: CI logs exist and at least one contains an ERROR marker.
what_changed: Nothing changes. grep prints the first ERROR match with nearby lines.
when_to_use: Use after finding which logs contain error markers.
when_not_to_use: Do not stop at the first match if the job continued and later failed for another reason.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A small context block around the first ERROR match.

shorts.title: Read around the error.
shorts.voiceover: The failing line is rarely enough. Print a few lines of context so the cause is visible.
shorts.caption: Use -C 3 for context.

linkedin.question: Do you read only the error line, or the context around it?

youtube.description: Use grep context flags to inspect nearby CI log lines around an error.

experiments.variant_a: The line before the error often explains the error.
experiments.variant_b: Print context before debugging a CI error.

title: List Failed Tests from JUnit XML
hook: The XML report already knows which tests failed.
problem: A test log is long, but the JUnit report contains structured failure records that can identify failed test cases quickly.
starting_state: CI has produced JUnit-style XML test reports.
what_changed: Nothing changes. grep prints failure and error records from XML reports.
when_to_use: Use when test logs are noisy but XML reports are available.
when_not_to_use: Do not use simple grep as a full XML parser when exact structured extraction is required.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: JUnit XML lines containing failure or error elements.

shorts.title: Ask JUnit what failed.
shorts.voiceover: When the test log is a wall of text, check the JUnit XML. Failed cases are already marked.
shorts.caption: JUnit reports are triage gold.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect JUnit XML directly when test logs get noisy?

youtube.description: Find failed tests in JUnit XML artifacts using shell tools.

experiments.variant_a: The XML report already knows which tests failed.
experiments.variant_b: Skip the noisy test log. Check JUnit.

title: Summarize Test Counts from Reports
hook: Before debugging a test failure, measure the blast radius.
problem: You need to know whether one test failed, many tests failed, or the whole suite crashed.
starting_state: JUnit-style XML files exist in the CI artifacts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The shell prints repeated test count attributes.
when_to_use: Use before diving into individual failures to understand test-suite scope.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this for exact aggregation across nested XML suites without a real parser.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Counts of test, failure, error, and skipped attributes.

shorts.title: Measure the test failure.
shorts.voiceover: Before reading stack traces, check whether CI lost one test, many tests, or the entire suite.
shorts.caption: Count failures first.

linkedin.question: Do you check test failure counts before reading stack traces?

youtube.description: Summarize JUnit test counts from CI artifact reports.

experiments.variant_a: Before debugging a test failure, measure the blast radius.
experiments.variant_b: Count CI test failures before reading traces.

title: Find Coverage Regression Lines
hook: Coverage failures usually say the threshold out loud.
problem: A coverage gate failed and you need the threshold, actual value, and affected file without scanning the full job output.
starting_state: CI logs or coverage summaries exist after a failed coverage job.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Matching coverage-related lines are printed.
when_to_use: Use when a coverage check fails but the relevant message is buried in build output.
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on text search alone for final coverage metrics if a structured report is available.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Coverage threshold and below-minimum lines from logs or reports.

shorts.title: Find the coverage threshold.
shorts.voiceover: Coverage gates usually print the expected value and the actual value. Search for those words first.
shorts.caption: Threshold first, blame later.

linkedin.question: When coverage fails, do you look for the threshold line first?

youtube.description: Find coverage threshold failures in CI logs and coverage artifacts.

experiments.variant_a: Coverage failures usually say the threshold out loud.

title: Find the Largest CI Artifacts
hook: A bloated artifact can explain a slow or failed pipeline.
problem: CI upload, cache, or packaging steps are slow or failing, and oversized artifacts may be the cause.
starting_state: A CI job has produced artifact files under an artifacts directory.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The shell prints the largest artifact files.
when_to_use: Use when artifact upload, cache restore, package creation, or disk usage looks suspicious.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete artifacts from this output alone; confirm which files are required.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The ten largest artifact files with byte sizes and paths.

commands: cd /lab/ci-artifacts && find artifacts -type f -printf '%s %p\n' | sort -nr | head -10
cd /lab/ci-artifacts && find artifacts -type f -printf '%s %p\n' | sort -nr | awk 'NR<=5 {printf "%.1f KB %s\n", $1/1024, $2}'

shorts.title: Find bloated artifacts fast.
shorts.voiceover: If CI is slow around upload or packaging, list artifacts by size. The biggest file often explains the delay.
shorts.caption: Sort artifacts by bytes.

linkedin.question: Have oversized artifacts ever slowed down your CI pipeline?

youtube.description: Use find and sort to locate the largest CI build artifacts.

experiments.variant_a: A bloated artifact can explain a slow or failed pipeline.

title: Check Whether Expected Build Outputs Exist
hook: The deploy failed because the build never produced the file.
problem: A deploy or packaging step cannot find files, and you need to verify the build output tree before debugging the deploy tool.
starting_state: A build artifact directory contains a dist output tree.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The build output files are listed.
when_to_use: Use when deploy, packaging, or static hosting steps complain about missing files.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume files are correct just because they exist; inspect sizes and content as needed.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted manifest of files under artifacts/dist.

shorts.title: Prove the build output exists.
shorts.voiceover: Before blaming deploy, check the build output. Missing files upstream become deploy failures downstream.
shorts.caption: List dist before deploy.

linkedin.question: Do you verify build outputs before debugging deploy failures?

youtube.description: Inspect CI build output manifests before debugging deployment failures.

experiments.variant_a: The deploy failed because the build never produced the file.

title: Detect Secret Leak Markers in Artifacts
hook: Artifacts are public more often than you think.
problem: Before publishing or sharing CI artifacts, you need a quick read-only scan for obvious secret-like markers.
starting_state: CI logs and artifacts are present locally.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Matching secret-like strings are printed for review.
when_to_use: Use before uploading, sharing, or attaching CI artifact bundles to tickets.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat this as a complete secret scanner; use a dedicated scanner for enforcement.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Any obvious secret marker lines found in logs or artifacts.

commands: cd /lab/ci-artifacts && grep -RInE 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY|SECRET|TOKEN|PRIVATE KEY|PASSWORD' artifacts logs | head -50
cd /lab/ci-artifacts && grep -RInE 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY|SECRET|TOKEN|PRIVATE KEY|PASSWORD' artifacts logs | cut -d: -f1 | sort -u

shorts.title: Scan artifacts before sharing.
shorts.voiceover: Before you attach CI artifacts to a ticket, search for obvious secret markers. It is fast and read-only.
shorts.caption: Do not leak debug secrets.

linkedin.question: Do you scan CI artifacts before sharing them outside the team?

youtube.description: Run a quick read-only scan for obvious secret markers in CI logs and artifacts.

experiments.variant_a: Artifacts are public more often than you think.

title: Find Tests That Passed After Rerun
hook: A green retry can still hide a flaky test.
problem: The pipeline passed after retry, but you need to identify tests that failed earlier in the same job.
command: grep -RInE 'rerun|retry|flaky|passed on retry|failed attempt' artifacts logs
starting_state: CI logs include retry or rerun output from test tooling.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Retry and flaky-test markers are printed.
when_to_use: Use after a successful retry or when CI instability is suspected.
when_not_to_use: Do not label a test flaky from one run alone; compare with history when possible.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Lines mentioning reruns, retries, flaky markers, or failed attempts.

commands: cd /lab/ci-artifacts && grep -RInE 'rerun|retry|flaky|passed on retry|failed attempt' artifacts logs
cd /lab/ci-artifacts && grep -RInE 'rerun|retry|flaky|passed on retry|failed attempt' artifacts logs | cut -d: -f1 | sort -u

shorts.title: Green retry is still a signal.
shorts.voiceover: If CI passed after retry, search the logs for rerun markers. Flaky tests leave tracks.
shorts.caption: Find hidden flaky tests.

linkedin.question: Do you investigate tests that only pass after retry?

youtube.description: Search CI logs for rerun and retry markers that indicate flaky tests.

experiments.variant_a: A green retry can still hide a flaky test.
experiments.variant_b: Find tests that only passed after retry.
